refashioned:
Let us turn now to generosity. It appears to be the virtuous mean with regard to wealth. The generous person is praised not for military courage, nor for temperance, nor for sound judgment, but specifically for how they give and receive wealth -- and especially for how they give.

By 'wealth' here we mean everything whose value can be measured in money. Prodigality and stinginess are the excess and deficiency with regard to wealth. We always apply the term 'stingy' to those who care about wealth more than they should. But the word 'prodigal' we sometimes use loosely, applying it to people who lack self-control and spend lavishly on self-indulgence. This is why prodigals are considered the worst sort of characters -- they combine multiple vices at once.

But this is not the proper use of the word. Strictly speaking, a 'prodigal' is someone with a single specific failing: wasting their resources. The prodigal is someone being ruined by their own hand, and the squandering of one's resources is a kind of self-destruction, since our very livelihood depends on having means.
elegant: Generosity is the virtuous mean concerning wealth, praised chiefly in the domain of giving and receiving -- especially giving. 'Wealth' means all things whose value is measured by money. Prodigality and stinginess are the excess and deficiency regarding wealth. Strictly, 'prodigality' means having a single fault: wasting one's substance. This is a kind of self-destruction, since life depends on possessing means.
summary: Generosity is the virtue that governs how we give and receive wealth. Prodigality (wasteful excess) and stinginess (excessive attachment to wealth) are its opposing vices, though the term 'prodigal' is often loosely applied to anyone who is self-indulgent with money.
distilled: Generosity is the virtuous mean in giving and receiving wealth, with prodigality and stinginess as its excess and deficiency.
notes: This chapter opens Aristotle's treatment of the particular virtues of character, following the general account of virtue as a mean in Book II. -- 'Liberality' (eleutheriotes) literally connotes the character of a free person (eleutheros), suggesting that generosity is bound up with freedom and noble birth. -- The distinction between the strict and loose senses of 'prodigal' (asotos) reflects Aristotle's characteristic method of beginning with common usage (endoxa) and then refining it. -- The claim that prodigality is self-destruction plays on the etymology of asotos ('un-saved' or 'ruined').

refashioned:
This, then, is the sense in which we use 'prodigality.' Now things that are useful can be used either well or badly. Wealth is a useful thing, and every useful thing is best employed by the person who possesses the relevant virtue. Therefore wealth will be best used by the person who has the virtue concerned with wealth -- and that is the generous person.

Spending and giving seem to constitute the use of wealth, while receiving and keeping are more a matter of possession. This is why generosity is more about giving to the right people than about taking from the right sources or refusing to take from wrong ones. For it is more characteristic of virtue to do good than to receive it, and more characteristic to act nobly than merely to avoid acting basely. Giving clearly involves doing good and acting nobly, while receiving involves having good done to you or simply not acting basely.

Moreover, gratitude goes to the giver, not to the person who merely refrains from taking. Praise, too, is more readily bestowed on givers. It is also easier to refrain from taking than to give, since people are more inclined to give away too little of their own than to take what belongs to others. Those who give are called generous, but those who simply refrain from taking are praised not for generosity but for justice -- while those who take are scarcely praised at all.

Among all people of virtuous character, the generous are perhaps the most beloved, because they are useful to others -- and this usefulness depends on their giving.
elegant: Wealth is best used by the person possessing the virtue concerned with it -- the generous person. Generosity is defined more by giving than by taking, because doing good is more characteristic of virtue than receiving it, and acting nobly matters more than merely avoiding what is base. Gratitude and praise flow to givers, not to those who merely refrain from taking. The generous are perhaps the most beloved of all virtuous people, because their giving makes them genuinely useful to others.
summary: Generosity is primarily about giving well rather than merely taking correctly. Giving is more virtuous than receiving because it involves actively doing good, which is why generous people are among the most beloved of all virtuous characters.
distilled: Generosity is defined more by giving than by receiving, because active goodness is more characteristic of virtue than passive avoidance of wrong.
notes: The priority of giving over taking reflects Aristotle's broader view that virtue is an active state (energeia) rather than a passive disposition; see NE II.1 and II.6. -- The claim that the generous are 'most loved' connects to the discussion of utility-friendships in NE VIII.3. -- The distinction between generosity (in giving) and justice (in not taking) anticipates the detailed treatment of justice in Book V.

refashioned:
Virtuous actions are noble and performed for the sake of what is noble. The generous person, like all virtuous people, will therefore give for the sake of what is noble, and will do so rightly -- giving to the right people, in the right amounts, at the right time, and with all the other qualifications that accompany right giving. Moreover, they will give with pleasure, or at least without pain. For what is done virtuously is pleasant or at the very least free from pain -- and giving done rightly will be the least painful of all.

But the person who gives to the wrong people, or not for the sake of what is noble but from some other motivation, will not be called generous but something else entirely.
elegant: The generous person gives for the sake of what is noble -- to the right people, in the right amounts, at the right time -- and does so with pleasure or at least without pain. Whoever gives to the wrong people or from the wrong motives deserves some other name than 'generous.'
summary: True generosity involves giving for noble reasons, to appropriate recipients, in appropriate amounts, with pleasure rather than reluctance. Giving from wrong motives or to wrong recipients is not generosity at all.
distilled: Genuine generosity means giving rightly for noble reasons and with pleasure, not merely parting with money.
notes: The insistence that virtuous action is 'for the sake of the noble' (tou kalou heneka) is a recurring requirement throughout the Ethics; see NE II.4 and III.7. -- The emphasis on pleasure accompanying virtuous action echoes NE II.3: the person who acts temperately with pleasure is truly temperate, while the person who does so with pain is not. -- The 'right people, right amounts, right time' formula is Aristotle's characteristic specification of the mean; see NE II.6 (1106b21).

refashioned:
Nor is the person who gives with pain truly generous. Such a person would prefer keeping the wealth to performing the noble act, and that preference is not characteristic of a generous person.

Equally, the generous person will not take from improper sources, since such taking is inconsistent with someone who places no excessive value on wealth. Nor will they be quick to ask for things, since it is not fitting for someone who confers benefits to accept them lightly.

But they will take from the right sources -- for example, from their own property -- not because receiving is noble in itself, but as a practical necessity, so that they have the means to give.
elegant: The person who gives with pain prefers wealth to noble action and is therefore not generous. Equally, the generous person will not take from wrong sources or ask readily for things, since that is inconsistent with being a benefactor. They will take only from proper sources -- such as their own property -- not as something noble, but as a necessity for having something to give.
summary: True generosity requires giving without reluctance, since preferring wealth to the noble act betrays a non-generous character. The generous person takes only from proper sources and only as a practical necessity to maintain the capacity to give.
distilled: The generous person gives without pain and takes only from proper sources as a practical means of sustaining their ability to give.
notes: The point that painful giving reveals a preference for wealth over nobility connects to Aristotle's view that the truly virtuous person's desires are properly ordered -- they take pleasure in what is genuinely good (NE I.8). -- The distinction between taking as a noble act and taking as a necessity introduces an asymmetry: generosity is fundamentally about the activity of giving, while receiving is merely instrumental.

refashioned:
The generous person will not neglect their own property, since it is the means by which they help others. And they will refrain from giving to just anyone and everyone, so that they have resources available to give to the right people, at the right time, and where giving is truly noble.

It is highly characteristic of the generous person to go to excess in giving, leaving too little for themselves. It is in their nature not to look to their own interests. Generosity is measured relative to one's means, for it resides not in the size of the gifts but in the character of the giver -- and character is assessed relative to the giver's resources. There is therefore nothing to prevent the person who gives less from being the more generous, if they have less to give.

Those who inherited their wealth are thought to be more generous than those who made it themselves. They have no experience of want, and besides, everyone is fonder of what they themselves have produced, just as parents are of their children and poets of their poems.

It is not easy for the generous person to be rich, since they are disposed neither to take nor to keep, but to give away, and they do not value wealth for its own sake but only as a means of giving.
elegant: The generous person manages their property so as to give to the right people at the right time, often giving too much and keeping too little -- for it is their nature not to look to themselves. Generosity resides not in the size of gifts but in the character of the giver relative to their means; a person with less who gives proportionately may be more generous than someone with more. Those who inherited wealth tend to be more generous since they have no experience of want. It is hard for the generous to become rich, since they are disposed to give rather than to take or keep.
summary: Generosity is measured relative to one's means, not by the absolute size of gifts. The generous person characteristically gives too much and keeps too little, which makes it difficult for them to accumulate wealth.
distilled: Generosity is measured by the giver's character relative to their means, not by the absolute size of what is given.
notes: The relativity of generosity to one's substance anticipates modern discussions of proportional vs. absolute giving. -- The observation that people are 'fonder of their own productions' (parents of children, poets of poems) recurs in NE IX.7, where Aristotle discusses why benefactors love their beneficiaries more than the reverse. -- The paradox that the generous find it hard to be rich connects to a broader Greek cultural tension between wealth and virtue visible in Solon's poetry and Plato's Republic.

refashioned:
This gives rise to the complaint commonly brought against fortune: that those who most deserve wealth get the least of it. But this outcome is not unreasonable. Just as with anything else, you cannot have wealth if you do not take pains to acquire it.

Yet the generous person will not give to the wrong people or at the wrong time, and so on, for that would mean acting contrary to generosity. If they spent on unworthy objects, they would have nothing left to spend on worthy ones. For as we have said, the generous person is one who spends according to their means and on the right objects. The person who exceeds this is prodigal.
elegant: It is no accident that the most generous are rarely the richest -- one cannot have wealth without effort to acquire it. Yet the generous person will not give to wrong recipients or at wrong times, for misplaced spending would leave nothing for worthy objects. The generous person spends according to their means on the right things; whoever exceeds this is prodigal.
summary: The generous person's difficulty in accumulating wealth is not truly unjust -- wealth requires effort to acquire. But generosity still requires discrimination: giving to the wrong people or at the wrong time leaves nothing for worthy recipients.
distilled: The generous are rarely rich because wealth requires acquisitiveness, but true generosity still demands giving discriminately to the right recipients.
notes: The complaint against fortune (tuche) reflects a common Greek concern about the apparent injustice of fortune's distribution of goods; compare Solon's poem to Croesus (Herodotus I.30-33). -- The remark that 'he cannot have wealth if he does not take pains' reflects Aristotle's general view that external goods, while partly subject to fortune, also require effort and practical wisdom (NE I.9-10).

refashioned:
This is why we do not call rulers prodigal: it seems impossible for them to give and spend beyond the scale of their vast possessions.

Since generosity is a mean with regard to giving and taking wealth, the generous person will both give and spend the right amounts on the right objects, in small things and great alike, and will do so with pleasure. They will also take the right amounts from the right sources. Since the virtue is a mean with regard to both giving and taking, the generous person will do both as they ought. For proper taking accompanies proper giving, and taking that does not match this standard is contrary to it. The kinds of giving and taking that accompany each other are naturally found together in the same person, while their opposites clearly are not.
elegant: We do not call rulers prodigal because their resources are too vast to exhaust through giving. Since generosity is a mean regarding both giving and taking, the generous person gives and spends the right amounts on the right objects with pleasure, and takes the right amounts from the right sources. Proper giving and proper taking accompany one another and are found together in the same person.
summary: Generosity involves getting both giving and taking right. Rulers cannot be prodigal because their resources are practically unlimited. Proper giving and proper taking naturally go together in the same person.
distilled: Generosity is a unified virtue of both right giving and right taking, each complementing the other in the same person.
notes: The remark about despots/rulers reflects the political realities of the Greek world, where tyrants commanded the resources of entire cities. -- The unity of giving and taking in generosity illustrates Aristotle's general principle that virtue is a single mean state, not two separate dispositions (NE II.6). -- The emphasis on pleasure in giving connects to the doctrine that the truly virtuous person takes pleasure in virtuous action (NE II.3).

refashioned:
If the generous person happens to spend in a way that is contrary to what is right and noble, they will feel pain -- but moderately and in the appropriate way. For it is the mark of virtue to be pleased and pained at the right objects and in the right manner.

Further, the generous person is easy to deal with in financial matters. They can be taken advantage of, since they place little value on money. They are more annoyed at having failed to spend where they should have than pained at having spent where they should not have. The generous person does not agree with the saying of Simonides.
elegant: When the generous person spends wrongly, they feel pain -- but moderately and appropriately, since virtue involves being pleased and pained at the right things in the right way. The generous person is easy to deal with in financial matters, even to the point of being exploitable, since they care little about money. They are more bothered by failing to give where they should than by having given where they should not.
summary: The generous person feels proportionate pain at financial mistakes and is easy to deal with in money matters. They are more troubled by missed opportunities to give than by unwise spending, and they do not share the miser's attachment to wealth.
distilled: The generous person regrets missed opportunities to give more than they regret unwise spending.
notes: Simonides of Ceos (c. 556-468 BCE) was a celebrated lyric poet notorious in Greek tradition for his love of money. Aristotle likely refers to the saying attributed to Simonides that it is better to be rich than wise, or his reported practice of charging fees for his poetry. -- The point about being 'pleased and pained at the right objects and in the right way' restates the core doctrine of NE II.3 and II.6. -- The idea that the generous person 'can be got the better of' shows that generosity involves a certain vulnerability -- a willingness to be disadvantaged rather than compromise one's character.

refashioned:
The prodigal person errs on these points as well: they are neither pleased nor pained at the right things or in the right way. This will become clearer as we continue.

We have established that prodigality and stinginess are excesses and deficiencies in two domains: giving and taking (counting spending as a form of giving). Prodigality involves excess in giving and deficiency in taking, while stinginess involves deficiency in giving and excess in taking -- except in trivial matters.
elegant: The prodigal errs by being pleased and pained at the wrong things and in the wrong way. Prodigality means excess in giving and deficiency in taking; stinginess means deficiency in giving and excess in taking, except in small matters.
summary: Prodigality and stinginess are opposing errors regarding giving and taking. The prodigal gives too much and takes too little; the stingy person gives too little and takes too much.
distilled: Prodigality is excess in giving paired with deficiency in taking, while stinginess is the reverse.
notes: The pairing of excess and deficiency across two dimensions (giving and taking) shows that the mean is not simply a midpoint but a complex balance across multiple parameters. -- This analysis exemplifies the doctrine of the mean from NE II.6-8, where Aristotle argues that each virtue is a mean between two vices. -- The qualification 'except in small things' for stinginess acknowledges that the stingy may appear reasonable in minor transactions.

refashioned:
The traits of prodigality are not often found combined, for it is difficult to give to everyone if you take from no one. Private individuals who give in this way soon exhaust their resources, and it is these people to whom the term 'prodigal' is properly applied. Yet such a person would seem to be considerably better than a stingy one.

The prodigal is easily cured, both by age and by poverty, and can thus move toward the mean. For they already have the characteristics of the generous person -- they give and refrain from taking -- though they do neither correctly or well. If they could be brought to do so through habituation or some other means, they would actually become generous, since they would then give to the right people and refrain from taking from wrong sources.

This is why the prodigal is not considered to have a bad character. Going to excess in giving while refraining from taking is not the mark of a wicked or ignoble person, but merely of a foolish one. The prodigal of this kind is thought far better than the stingy person, both for the reasons already given and because they benefit many people, while the stingy person benefits no one -- not even themselves.
elegant: Pure prodigality is rare, since giving to everyone requires resources that private persons quickly exhaust. Yet the prodigal is far better than the stingy: they already possess the generous person's instinct to give and not to take, though not correctly. Age, poverty, or proper habituation can cure them, redirecting their natural generosity toward the right recipients. Excessive giving is the mark of a fool, not a wicked person, and the prodigal at least benefits others, while the stingy person benefits no one.
summary: The prodigal is far preferable to the stingy person because they share the generous person's disposition toward giving and can be reformed through experience or training. Excessive giving marks foolishness, not wickedness, and at least benefits others.
distilled: The prodigal is closer to generosity than the stingy person because they already possess the instinct to give and can be reformed.
notes: The idea that prodigality is curable while stinginess is harder to reform anticipates the discussion in 4.12. -- The role of habituation (ethismos) in redirecting the prodigal's impulses connects to Aristotle's foundational claim in NE II.1 that moral virtue comes through habit. -- The claim that the prodigal benefits many while the stingy benefits none introduces a consequentialist-sounding consideration within Aristotle's virtue framework. -- The asymmetry between the vices (one closer to virtue than the other) is a pattern Aristotle identifies for many virtues; see NE II.8.

refashioned:
But most prodigal people, as we said, also take from improper sources, and in this respect they are stingy. They develop the habit of taking because they want to spend but cannot do so easily, since their resources quickly run out. They are forced to find means elsewhere. At the same time, because they have no concern for honour, they take recklessly from any source -- their appetite is for giving, and they do not care how or where the means come from.

Consequently, their giving is not truly generous: it is not noble, it does not aim at nobility, and it is not done in the right way. Sometimes they make rich people who should be poor, give nothing to people of respectable character, and lavish resources on flatterers or those who provide them with some other pleasure.

This is also why most such prodigals are self-indulgent. They spend freely and waste money on their indulgences, and they gravitate toward pleasures because they do not live with a view to what is noble.
elegant: Most prodigals also take from wrong sources -- a form of stinginess. Wanting to spend but having exhausted their resources, they take recklessly from anywhere, caring nothing for honour. Their giving therefore lacks nobility: they enrich the undeserving, ignore the respectable, and lavish on flatterers. Most become self-indulgent, wasting money on pleasures because they do not orient their lives toward what is noble.
summary: Most prodigals combine excessive giving with taking from wrong sources, since they exhaust their resources and must find money wherever they can. Their giving lacks genuine nobility because it is directed at flatterers and pleasure-providers rather than deserving recipients.
distilled: Most prodigals degenerate into taking from wrong sources to fund indiscriminate giving, producing a giving that is lavish but ignoble.
notes: The degeneration of prodigality into a combination of wasteful giving and dishonourable taking shows how one vice can compound into another. -- The mention of flatterers (kolakes) connects to Aristotle's treatment of flattery as a vice opposed to proper social friendliness in NE IV.6. -- The phrase 'do not live with a view to what is noble' articulates a key Aristotelian criterion: the truly virtuous person orients their entire life toward the kalon (noble/fine). -- The pattern where prodigals enrich the unworthy while ignoring the worthy resonates with broader Greek concerns about the social disruption caused by misplaced wealth.

refashioned:
The prodigal, then, turns into what we have described if left without guidance. But with proper care, they will arrive at the intermediate and right state.

Stinginess, however, is both incurable -- for old age and every form of incapacity is thought to make people stingier -- and more deeply innate in human nature than prodigality, since most people are fonder of acquiring money than of giving it away. Stinginess is also wide-ranging and takes many forms, since there seem to be many varieties of it.
elegant: With guidance the prodigal can reach the virtuous mean, but stinginess is incurable. Age and infirmity make people stingier, and the vice is more natural than prodigality since most people prefer acquiring money to giving it away. Stinginess is also wide-ranging, taking many forms.
summary: Prodigality can be corrected through guidance and experience, but stinginess is essentially incurable and more deeply rooted in human nature, since most people naturally prefer getting money to giving it away.
distilled: Prodigality is curable with guidance, but stinginess is incurable because the love of acquiring is more deeply rooted in human nature.
notes: The incurability of stinginess contrasts sharply with Aristotle's usual optimism about moral development through habituation (NE II.1). -- The observation that most people prefer acquiring to giving anticipates Hobbes and later pessimistic accounts of human nature. -- The claim that old age increases stinginess reflects a commonplace of Greek literature; compare Aristophanes' portrayal of elderly misers. -- The 'many forms' of stinginess sets up the detailed taxonomy in 4.13-4.14.

refashioned:
For stinginess consists of two things -- deficiency in giving and excess in taking -- and is not found in its complete form in everyone. Sometimes the two are separated: some people go to excess in taking, while others fall short in giving.

Those called by such names as 'miserly,' 'tight-fisted,' and 'stingy' all fall short in giving but do not covet other people's possessions or wish to take them. In some cases this is due to a kind of honesty and desire to avoid what is disgraceful. Some appear to hoard their money -- or at least claim to -- so that they will never be forced into doing something dishonourable. To this class belongs the penny-pincher and everyone of that sort, named for their excessive unwillingness to part with anything.

Others refrain from taking what belongs to others out of fear, reasoning that if they take from others, it becomes difficult to prevent others from taking from them. They are therefore content neither to take nor to give.
elegant: Stinginess has two components -- deficiency in giving and excess in taking -- which are not always combined. Some are merely tight-fisted: they give too little but do not covet others' possessions. Some hoard from a kind of caution, wanting never to be forced into dishonourable actions. Others avoid taking from fear of reciprocal loss, so they neither take nor give.
summary: Stinginess takes different forms: some people only fail at giving while others also take excessively. The merely tight-fisted refrain from giving out of excessive caution or fear, sometimes even claiming moral reasons for their hoarding.
distilled: Stinginess is not monolithic but divides into those who merely fail to give and those who also take excessively, each driven by different motives.
notes: The Greek terms translated as 'miserly,' 'close,' and 'stingy' (kimix, gliskhros, kumminopristēs) are colourful colloquial terms. The 'cheeseparer' (kumminopristēs, literally 'cumin-splitter') refers to someone so mean they would split a cumin seed rather than part with a whole one. -- The observation that some hoard from a sense of honour introduces moral complexity into what might seem a straightforward vice. -- Aristotle's taxonomic approach to vice, distinguishing varieties by their motivational structure, is characteristic of his empirical method in ethics.

refashioned:
Others go to excess in taking, willing to take anything from any source. These include those who pursue degrading trades -- pimps and their like -- and those who lend small sums at high interest rates. All of them take more than they should and from improper sources. What they share is a sordid love of gain: they endure a bad reputation for the sake of profit, and small profit at that.

We do not call those who make large gains from wrong sources 'stingy' -- for example, rulers who sack cities and plunder temples. These we call wicked, impious, and unjust instead. But the gambler, the mugger, and the highway robber do belong to the class of the stingy, since they act from a sordid love of gain. Both the robber and the gambler ply their trade and endure its disgrace for the sake of profit. The robber faces the greatest dangers for the sake of plunder, while the gambler profits at the expense of friends, to whom he ought to be giving. Both are willing to profit from wrong sources, and so both exhibit a sordid love of gain. All such forms of taking are therefore species of stinginess.
elegant: Some take anything from any source: those in degrading trades, usurers, and the like. They share a sordid love of gain, enduring dishonour for small profit. We do not call rulers who plunder cities 'stingy' but rather wicked and impious. However, gamblers, muggers, and robbers do belong to stinginess: they endure disgrace and danger for sordid gain, profiting from wrong sources. All such forms of taking are species of stinginess.
summary: Those who take from any source for gain -- including people in degrading trades, usurers, gamblers, and robbers -- are all species of stinginess driven by sordid love of gain. Large-scale plunderers like tyrants are not stingy but wicked on a different scale entirely.
distilled: Sordid love of small gain -- whether through degrading trades, usury, gambling, or robbery -- is the common root of all varieties of excessive taking.
notes: The mention of 'pimps' (pornoboskoi) and usurers reflects widespread Greek contempt for certain occupations; lending at interest was viewed with suspicion across much of the ancient world. -- The distinction between stinginess and outright wickedness (in the case of tyrants sacking cities) shows that Aristotle differentiates vices by scale and kind. -- 'Spoiling temples' was one of the gravest offenses in Greek religion and law. -- The gambler who profits 'from friends to whom he ought to be giving' connects to the discussion of friendship and reciprocity in NE VIII-IX.

refashioned:
It is natural that stinginess is described as the contrary of generosity, for not only is it a greater evil than prodigality, but people err in this direction far more often than in the direction of prodigality as we have described it.

So much, then, for generosity and the vices opposed to it.
elegant: Stinginess is rightly called the true contrary of generosity: it is both a greater evil and a far more common error than prodigality.
summary: Stinginess is the true opposite of generosity because it is both worse and more common than prodigality.
distilled: Stinginess is the true opposite of generosity -- a greater evil and a more common human failing than prodigality.
notes: Aristotle's claim that the vice of deficiency (stinginess) is more opposed to generosity than the vice of excess (prodigality) illustrates a pattern noted in NE II.8: for each virtue, one of the two opposing vices is more contrary to it than the other. -- The chapter on generosity (NE IV.1) is among the longest treatments of any single virtue, reflecting the cultural importance of wealth and giving in Athenian social life. -- This closing formula is characteristic of Aristotle's transitional passages between topics.

refashioned:
It seems fitting to discuss magnificence next, for this too appears to be a virtue concerned with wealth. But unlike generosity, it does not extend to all financial actions -- only to those involving expenditure, and in these it surpasses generosity in scale. For as the very name suggests, magnificence is a fitting expenditure on a grand scale.

But scale is relative: the expense of equipping a warship is not the same as that of funding a sacred embassy. What counts as fitting depends on the person, the circumstances, and the purpose. Someone who spends appropriately on modest or middling matters is not called magnificent -- for instance, someone who can say 'many a gift I gave the wanderer.' Only the person who does so on a grand scale is magnificent.

The magnificent person is always generous, but the generous person is not necessarily magnificent. The deficiency of this virtue is called niggardliness. The excess is called vulgarity or tastelessness -- not because the amount spent is too great for the right objects, but because the spending is showy in the wrong circumstances and in the wrong manner. We will discuss these vices in more detail later.
elegant: Magnificence is a virtue concerning wealth, but only in the domain of large-scale expenditure -- surpassing generosity in scope. Scale is relative to the person, circumstances, and purpose: equipping a warship differs from funding an embassy. Only those who spend fittingly on grand occasions deserve the name. Every magnificent person is generous, but not every generous person is magnificent. The deficiency is niggardliness; the excess is vulgarity -- not spending too much on right objects, but spending showily on wrong occasions.
summary: Magnificence is the virtue of spending fittingly on a grand scale. It differs from generosity in being limited to large expenditures. Its deficiency is niggardliness and its excess is vulgar showiness.
distilled: Magnificence is the virtue of spending fittingly and tastefully on a grand scale, distinct from ordinary generosity in its scope.
notes: The Greek term megaloprepeia literally means 'greatness of what is fitting' (megas + prepei). -- The quotation 'many a gift I gave the wanderer' is from Homer's Odyssey (XVII.420), spoken by the disguised Odysseus. -- The trireme (triērēs) was the principal warship of Greek navies; equipping one (trierarchy) was among the most expensive public duties (leitourgiai) imposed on wealthy Athenians. -- Sacred embassies (theōriai) were delegations sent to represent a city at major religious festivals like the Olympic Games. -- The distinction between magnificence and generosity parallels the structure of NE IV generally, moving from common virtues to those requiring special circumstances or resources.

refashioned:
The magnificent person is like an artist: they can discern what is fitting and spend large sums with taste. For as we said at the beginning, a state of character is determined by its activities and their objects. The magnificent person's expenditures are large and fitting, and so are the results. There will be a great expenditure that is fitting to its result. The result should therefore be worthy of the expense, and the expense worthy of the result -- or should even exceed it.

The magnificent person will make such expenditures for honour's sake, since this motivation is common to all the virtues. Moreover, they will do so gladly and lavishly, for meticulous cost-calculation is a sign of niggardliness. They will think about how to make the result as beautiful and becoming as possible, rather than about how cheaply it can be produced.

It follows that the magnificent person must also be generous.
elegant: The magnificent person is like an artist who perceives what is fitting and spends large sums with taste. Their results must be worthy of the expense, and the expense worthy of the result -- or even exceed it. They spend for honour's sake, gladly and lavishly, since meticulous cost-calculation is niggardly. Their aim is beauty and fittingness, not economy. The magnificent person must therefore also be generous.
summary: The magnificent person has an artist's eye for what is fitting, spending large sums to produce beautiful and worthy results. They prioritize beauty and honour over economy, since penny-pinching is contrary to magnificence.
distilled: Magnificence resembles artistry: it seeks the most beautiful and fitting result, not the cheapest, spending gladly for the sake of honour.
notes: The comparison to an artist (technites) is significant: Aristotle sees practical wisdom (phronesis) as analogous to craft knowledge but operating in the domain of action. -- 'A state of character is determined by its activities and their objects' restates the principle from NE II.1-2 that character is formed through action. -- The contrast between beauty and cheapness reflects the Greek aristocratic ideal that the kalon (noble/beautiful) transcends mere utility. -- The requirement that the magnificent person also be generous establishes a hierarchy: magnificence includes generosity as a necessary component.

refashioned:
For the generous person also spends what they ought and as they ought. It is precisely in these matters that the greatness implied in the name 'magnificent' -- its grandeur, as it were -- is manifested, since generosity operates in the same domain. At an equal level of expense, the magnificent person will produce a more splendid result.

For a possession and a work of art do not share the same standard of excellence. The most valuable possession is whatever is worth the most -- for example, gold. But the most valuable work of art is what is great and beautiful, since contemplating such a work inspires admiration, and magnificence aims at precisely this. A work possesses the excellence of magnificence when it involves magnitude.

Magnificence belongs to the kind of expenditures we call honourable -- those connected with the gods (votive offerings, temple buildings, and sacrifices), and similarly with any form of religious observance, as well as those that are fitting objects of public-spirited ambition: when people feel they ought to equip a chorus or a warship or host a public feast in brilliant fashion.
elegant: The generous person spends rightly, but the magnificent person, at the same expense, produces a more splendid result. Possessions and works of art have different standards: the best possession is worth the most money, but the best work is great and beautiful, inspiring admiration -- and this is what magnificence aims at. Magnificence belongs to honourable expenditures: offerings to the gods, temple buildings, sacrifices, religious observances, and public-spirited displays like equipping a chorus or warship or hosting a feast brilliantly.
summary: Magnificence exceeds generosity by producing grander results from the same expense. Its standard of excellence is not monetary value but beauty and grandeur, and it applies to honourable public and religious expenditures.
distilled: Magnificence aims at producing what is great and beautiful, not merely what is expensive, in the domain of honourable public expenditure.
notes: The distinction between the value of a possession (monetary worth) and the excellence of a work of art (beauty and grandeur) anticipates later aesthetic theory. -- Votive offerings (anathēmata), temple construction, and sacrifice were the primary forms of religious expenditure in ancient Greece. -- Equipping a chorus (chorēgia) for dramatic festivals and a trireme (triērarchia) were among the most prestigious and expensive public duties (leitourgiai) required of wealthy Athenians. -- The connection between magnificence and public benefaction reflects the Greek culture of euergetism (benefaction) where wealthy citizens competed in public-spirited display.

refashioned:
But in all cases, as we have said, we must consider the agent as well: who they are and what resources they have. For the expenditure should be worthy of their means, and should suit not only the result but also the person producing it. This is why a poor person cannot be magnificent -- they lack the means to spend large sums fittingly. The poor person who tries is a fool, since they are spending beyond what can be expected of them and beyond what is appropriate. Only right expenditure is virtuous.

Great expenditure is fitting for those who have suitable means -- whether acquired through their own efforts, inherited, or gained through connections -- as well as for people of high birth or reputation, since all these things bring greatness and prestige with them. The magnificent person is primarily of this sort, and magnificence is displayed in the kinds of expenditure we have described, for these are the greatest and most honourable.

Among private occasions, the most suitable are those that happen only once -- such as a wedding or anything of that kind -- as well as whatever interests the whole city or people of standing. Receiving and sending off foreign guests, and exchanging gifts, are also fitting occasions. The magnificent person spends not on themselves but on public purposes, and gifts bear a certain resemblance to votive offerings.

A magnificent person will also furnish their house in a manner suited to their wealth, since even a house is a kind of public ornament, and will prefer to spend on works that are lasting, for these are the most beautiful. In every category of expenditure, they will spend what is becoming -- for the same things are not suitable for gods and for humans, nor in a temple and in a tomb.

Since each expenditure may be great of its kind, and what is absolutely most magnificent is great expenditure on a great object, while what is magnificent in a particular context is what is great in those circumstances -- and since greatness in the work differs from greatness in the expense (for the most beautiful ball or bottle is magnificent as a gift to a child, though its price is small and modest) -- it is therefore characteristic of the magnificent person, whatever kind of result they are producing, to produce it magnificently, in a way not easily surpassed, and to make it worthy of the expenditure.
elegant: The expenditure must suit the agent's means and standing: a poor person cannot be magnificent, and attempting it is foolish. Magnificence belongs to those with ample resources through effort, inheritance, or connections, and to those of high birth or reputation. The most fitting occasions are once-in-a-lifetime events, public celebrations, hospitality to foreign guests, and religious offerings. The magnificent person spends on public purposes, prefers lasting works, and recognizes that fittingness varies by context -- what suits a temple differs from what suits a tomb, and a beautiful ball may be a magnificent gift for a child though its price is small. In every case, the magnificent person produces a result not easily surpassed.
summary: Magnificence requires both suitable means and fitting occasions. It is proper to the wealthy and well-born, directed at public purposes and lasting works. What counts as magnificent is relative to context -- even a small gift can be magnificent if perfectly suited to its recipient and occasion.
distilled: Magnificence requires matching great expenditure to great occasions and suitable means, while recognizing that fittingness is always relative to context.
notes: The claim that a poor person cannot be magnificent reflects Aristotle's view that certain virtues require external goods (NE I.8). -- The idea that a house is 'a sort of public ornament' reflects the Greek understanding of the wealthy household as a civic institution. -- The relativity of magnificence (a beautiful ball is magnificent as a gift to a child) shows sophisticated sensitivity to context, paralleling the doctrine that the mean is relative to the individual (NE II.6). -- Foreign guests (xenoi) and gift exchange played a crucial role in Greek diplomacy and aristocratic social networks. -- The preference for lasting works connects to Aristotle's broader valuation of what endures; compare his ranking of theoretical contemplation in NE X.7.

refashioned:
Such, then, is the magnificent person. The one who goes to excess and is vulgar overspends, as we said, by spending beyond what is appropriate. On minor occasions they spend lavishly and make a tasteless display -- for instance, hosting a club dinner on the scale of a wedding banquet, or when providing a chorus for a comedy, bringing the performers onstage in purple robes, as they do at Megara. They do all this not for honour's sake but to show off their wealth, thinking they will be admired for it. Where they should spend much, they spend little; where they should spend little, they spend much.

The niggardly person, on the other hand, falls short in everything. After spending very large sums, they will ruin the beauty of the result to save a trifle. Whatever they are doing, they hesitate and calculate how they might spend the least, lamenting even that amount and always believing they are doing everything on a grander scale than they should.

These states of character are vices, yet they do not bring great disgrace because they are neither harmful to others nor especially unseemly.
elegant: The vulgar person overspends on minor occasions -- hosting club dinners like weddings, dressing comedy choruses in purple -- not for honour but to display wealth. Where much should be spent, they spend little, and vice versa. The niggardly person ruins great expenditures over trifles, always calculating the minimum and lamenting even that. Both are vices, but neither brings great disgrace since they harm no one and are not deeply unseemly.
summary: The vulgar person spends lavishly on wrong occasions to show off, while the niggardly person ruins worthy efforts by cutting corners over trifles. Both are vices but relatively harmless ones.
distilled: Vulgarity is showy spending on wrong occasions to impress, while niggardliness ruins worthy endeavours by obsessing over trifling savings.
notes: Megara, a city between Athens and Corinth, was known for its lavish theatrical productions that Athenians considered tasteless -- an early example of cultural snobbery between rival Greek cities. -- Purple dye, extracted from murex snails, was among the most expensive commodities in the ancient world; using it for a comedy chorus would be absurdly extravagant. -- The 'club dinner' (eranos) was a common form of Greek social gathering where members contributed equally to a shared meal. -- The concluding remark that these vices 'do not bring disgrace' because they are not harmful introduces a consequentialist nuance: vices that harm only the agent are judged less severely than those harming others.

refashioned:
Magnanimity, even from its name, seems to be concerned with great things. What sort of great things is the first question we must answer. It makes no difference whether we consider the state of character or the person who embodies it.

The magnanimous person is one who thinks themselves worthy of great things and is in fact worthy of them. Someone who makes such claims beyond their actual merit is a fool -- but no truly virtuous person is foolish or silly. The magnanimous person, then, is as we have described.

The person who is worthy of little and thinks themselves worthy of little is moderate, but not magnanimous; for magnanimity implies greatness, just as beauty implies a good-sized body. Small people may be neat and well-proportioned, but they cannot be called beautiful.

The person who thinks themselves worthy of great things while being unworthy of them is vain -- though not everyone who overestimates their worth is vain. The person who thinks themselves worthy of less than they actually deserve is unduly humble, whether their true merits are great, moderate, or small -- so long as their self-assessment falls below their actual worth. The person whose merits are genuinely great seems the most unduly humble of all; for what would they have claimed if their merits had been less?

The magnanimous person, then, is extreme with respect to the greatness of their claims, but occupies the mean with respect to their correctness -- for they claim what accords with their actual merits, while others go to excess or fall short.
elegant: Magnanimity concerns great things. The magnanimous person thinks themselves worthy of great things and is worthy of them. One who overestimates their merit is vain; one who underestimates it is unduly humble -- especially blameworthy when their actual merits are great. Just as beauty requires a certain stature, magnanimity requires genuine greatness. The magnanimous person is extreme in the scale of their claims but occupies the mean in their accuracy, since they claim exactly what they deserve.
summary: Magnanimity is the virtue of accurately assessing one's own great worth. The magnanimous person claims great things and genuinely deserves them, while the vain person overclaims and the unduly humble person underclaims. Magnanimity requires actual greatness, just as beauty requires physical stature.
distilled: Magnanimity is the accurate self-assessment of genuine greatness -- claiming exactly what one deserves, neither more nor less.
notes: Megalopsychia (literally 'greatness of soul') is one of Aristotle's most distinctive and controversial virtues, with no easy modern equivalent. -- The analogy between magnanimity and physical beauty illustrates Aristotle's characteristic method of reasoning by analogy. -- The claim that magnanimity is 'extreme in greatness but mean in correctness' shows a sophisticated application of the doctrine of the mean: the virtue is not simply the midpoint between two extremes but the state that gets things right. -- This chapter has generated enormous philosophical discussion. Aquinas reinterpreted magnanimity in Christian terms as proper confidence in God-given abilities; Hume's 'great man' draws on this portrait; Nietzsche's Übermensch has often been compared (and contrasted) with Aristotle's magnanimous person.

refashioned:
If, then, the magnanimous person deserves and claims great things, and above all the greatest things, they will be concerned with one thing in particular. 'Desert' is relative to external goods, and the greatest of external goods is that which we render to the gods, which people of high position most seek, and which is the prize awarded for the noblest deeds. This is honour -- surely the greatest of external goods.

Honour and dishonour, therefore, are the objects with which the magnanimous person is primarily concerned. And even without formal argument, it is evident that magnanimous people are concerned with honour, for it is honour that they chiefly claim -- but in proportion to their merits.

The unduly humble person falls short both relative to their own merits and relative to the magnanimous person's claims. The vain person goes to excess relative to their own merits but does not exceed the claims of the magnanimous person.
elegant: The magnanimous person is concerned above all with honour, the greatest of external goods -- what we render to the gods, what the prominent most desire, and what is awarded for the noblest deeds. They claim honour in proportion to their merits. The unduly humble fall short of both their own merits and the magnanimous person's standard; the vain exceed their own merits but not the magnanimous person's claims.
summary: Honour is the greatest external good and the primary concern of the magnanimous person, who claims it in proportion to genuine merit. The unduly humble undervalue themselves, while the vain overvalue themselves but still fall short of true magnanimity.
distilled: Honour is the greatest external good, and magnanimity is the virtue of claiming it in exact proportion to one's genuine merit.
notes: The identification of honour (timē) as the greatest external good is crucial to Aristotle's ethics, though he ultimately ranks contemplation above honour in NE X.7-8. -- In NE I.5, Aristotle criticizes the 'life of honour' as too dependent on those who bestow it, yet here he treats honour as the fitting reward for genuine excellence. -- The hierarchy 'what we render to the gods, what the prominent seek, the prize for noblest deeds' places honour in its full Greek cultural context, where divine honours, political prestige, and athletic/military prizes formed a continuum.

refashioned:
Now the magnanimous person, since they deserve the most, must be good in the highest degree. For the better person always deserves more, and the best person deserves most. Therefore the truly magnanimous person must be good.

Greatness in every virtue would seem characteristic of the magnanimous person. It would be utterly unbecoming for them to flee from danger with arms flailing, or to wrong another person. For why would someone to whom nothing seems great do anything disgraceful? If we examine each point, we see the complete absurdity of a magnanimous person who is not good.

Nor would they be worthy of honour if they were bad, for honour is the prize of virtue and is awarded to the good. Magnanimity, then, seems to be a kind of crown of the virtues. It makes them greater, and it cannot exist without them.
elegant: Since the magnanimous person deserves the most, they must be good in the highest degree -- the better person always deserves more. Greatness in every virtue is characteristic of them: they would never flee danger or wrong another, since nothing seems great enough to tempt them to act disgracefully. Nor could they deserve honour without being good, since honour is the prize of virtue. Magnanimity is therefore a crown of the virtues: it amplifies them and cannot exist without them.
summary: The magnanimous person must possess all the virtues, since they deserve the highest honour and honour is the prize of virtue. Magnanimity is the 'crown of the virtues' -- it amplifies all other virtues and cannot exist without them.
distilled: Magnanimity is the crown of the virtues: it presupposes and amplifies all other excellences of character.
notes: The metaphor of magnanimity as the 'crown' (kosmos) of the virtues makes it the supreme expression of moral excellence short of the intellectual virtue of wisdom. -- The argument that the magnanimous person must be comprehensively virtuous relies on the 'unity of the virtues' thesis developed in NE VI.13: one cannot truly possess any virtue without possessing all of them. -- The claim that the magnanimous person would never act disgracefully 'since nothing seems great enough' inverts the usual relationship between temptation and virtue -- their greatness of soul itself makes temptation irrelevant. -- This passage is one of the most admired and debated in the Ethics; medieval Christian thinkers struggled to reconcile this self-regarding virtue with the Christian virtue of humility.

refashioned:
It is therefore difficult to be truly magnanimous, for it is impossible without nobility and goodness of character.

The magnanimous person is concerned chiefly with honours and dishonours. At great honours conferred by good people, they will be moderately pleased, feeling that they are receiving what is their own or even less than their due -- for no honour can truly match perfect virtue. Yet they will accept it, since those conferring it have nothing greater to give. But honour from ordinary people or on trivial grounds they will utterly disdain, since it is not what they deserve. They will likewise disdain dishonour, since in their case it cannot be just.

The magnanimous person, then, is concerned primarily with honour, as we have said. Yet they will also conduct themselves with moderation toward wealth, power, and every kind of good or bad fortune. They will be neither overjoyed by good fortune nor crushed by misfortune.
elegant: True magnanimity is difficult, requiring nobility and goodness of character. The magnanimous person is moderately pleased by great honours from worthy people -- accepting what no honour can fully match, since nothing equals perfect virtue -- but utterly disdains honour from trivial sources and unjust dishonour alike. They also bear wealth, power, and all fortune with equanimity, neither overjoyed by success nor crushed by adversity.
summary: True magnanimity is rare because it demands comprehensive virtue. The magnanimous person accepts great honours from worthy sources with moderate pleasure, disdains trivial recognition and unjust criticism, and maintains equanimity through all changes of fortune.
distilled: The magnanimous person accepts deserved honour with quiet composure and meets all fortune -- good or bad -- with equanimity.
notes: The claim that 'no honour can truly match perfect virtue' reveals a tension in Aristotle's account: if honour is the greatest external good but cannot adequately reward the greatest virtue, then external goods are fundamentally insufficient for the best human life -- pointing toward the superiority of contemplation argued in NE X. -- The equanimity toward fortune connects to the portrait of the happy person in NE I.10-11, who is 'not easily dislodged from happiness.' -- This portrait anticipates Stoic ideals of apatheia (freedom from passion), though Aristotle allows moderate emotional responses where the Stoics would eliminate them.

refashioned:
For the magnanimous person does not treat even honour as if it were something supremely important. Power and wealth are desirable for the sake of honour -- at least those who possess them wish to gain honour through them. For someone to whom even honour is a small thing, these other goods must be smaller still.

This is why magnanimous people are thought to be disdainful.
elegant: The magnanimous person does not treat even honour as supremely important. Since power and wealth are desired for the sake of honour, and honour itself is a small thing to the magnanimous, these goods must be smaller still. Hence magnanimous people appear disdainful.
summary: Since the magnanimous person regards even honour -- the greatest external good -- as relatively unimportant, they necessarily regard power and wealth, which are sought for honour's sake, as even less significant. This produces their characteristic air of disdain.
distilled: The magnanimous person's disdain arises naturally because even honour, the greatest external good, seems relatively small to them.
notes: The hierarchy of external goods -- honour above wealth and power, which are desired for its sake -- is important for understanding Aristotle's axiology. -- The characteristic disdain (hyperoptikos) of the magnanimous person has been both admired and criticized across philosophical history. Christian ethics found this quality troubling; Hume, by contrast, valued proper pride. -- This passage shows how Aristotle derives behavioral traits (disdain) from the internal logic of the virtue itself, rather than treating them as arbitrary features.

refashioned:
The goods of fortune are also thought to contribute to magnanimity. For the well-born are considered worthy of honour, as are those who enjoy power or wealth, since they occupy a superior position, and whatever holds a superiority in something good is held in greater honour. Such advantages do therefore make people more disposed toward magnanimity, for they are honoured by some people for possessing them.

But in truth, only the good person is genuinely worthy of honour. The person who has both advantages -- external goods and virtue -- is thought most worthy of honour. But those who possess such goods without virtue are neither justified in making great claims nor entitled to be called magnanimous, for these things require perfect virtue.

Such people do, however, become disdainful and insolent. For without virtue it is not easy to bear the goods of fortune gracefully. Unable to bear them well and thinking themselves superior, they look down on others while themselves doing as they please. They imitate the magnanimous person without actually being like them, and they do this wherever they can -- so they do not act virtuously, but they do despise others.

The magnanimous person despises justly, because they judge truly. But the many despise at random.
elegant: Fortune's goods -- birth, power, wealth -- dispose people toward magnanimity by earning them honour. But only the virtuous truly deserve honour; external goods without virtue justify neither great claims nor the name 'magnanimous.' Those who have fortune's goods without virtue become disdainful and insolent: unable to bear prosperity gracefully, they imitate the magnanimous person's contempt without possessing the virtue behind it. The magnanimous person despises justly, judging truly; the many despise at random.
summary: External goods like birth, wealth, and power can support magnanimity but cannot substitute for virtue. Those who have fortune's goods without virtue become insolent imitators of magnanimity, despising others randomly rather than with the justified discernment of the truly magnanimous.
distilled: External goods without virtue produce a counterfeit magnanimity -- unjustified disdain that imitates the form of greatness without its substance.
notes: The distinction between justified and random disdain is crucial: magnanimity is not simple arrogance but an accurate response to one's genuine standing. -- The claim that fortune's goods without virtue make people insolent (hubristēs) connects to the Greek concept of hubris, the arrogance that invites divine punishment in tragedy. -- This analysis resonates with Plato's portrayal of the tyrannical soul in Republic VIII-IX, where the person with power but without virtue becomes the worst of all. -- The observation that the many 'imitate' the magnanimous person prefigures later discussions of inauthenticity in moral character.

refashioned:
The magnanimous person does not rush into minor dangers or seek danger for its own sake, because they value few things. But they will face great dangers, and in danger they are unsparing of their life, knowing that there are conditions under which life is not worth living.

They are the sort of person who confers benefits but is ashamed to receive them, for conferring benefits is the mark of a superior, while receiving them marks an inferior. They tend to return benefits in greater measure, so that the original benefactor ends up indebted to them and becomes the one who has gained from the exchange.

Magnanimous people seem to remember the services they have performed but not those they have received -- for the recipient of a service is inferior to the one who performed it, and the magnanimous person wishes to be the superior party. They hear of their own services with pleasure and those done for them with displeasure. This, it seems, is why Thetis did not mention to Zeus the services she had done him, and why the Spartans recounted to the Athenians not the services they had rendered but only those they had received.

It is also characteristic of the magnanimous person to ask for nothing or hardly anything, but to give help readily. They are dignified toward people of high position and good fortune, but unassuming toward those of the middle class. For it is difficult and therefore impressive to be superior to the former, but easy to be superior to the latter -- and bearing oneself loftily over the great is no sign of bad breeding, while doing so among humble people is as vulgar as making a show of strength against the weak.

The magnanimous person does not pursue the things commonly valued or the areas where others excel. They are unhurried and hold back except when great honour or a great achievement is at stake, undertaking few actions but those of great significance and note.

They must also be open in their hatreds and their loves, for concealing one's feelings -- caring more for what others think than for truth -- is a coward's part. They speak and act openly, being free in speech because they are contemptuous of the opinion of others, and they tell the truth, except when they speak with irony to the common crowd.

They cannot arrange their life around another person, unless that person is a friend, for doing so is slavish -- and this is why all flatterers are servile, and all people lacking in self-respect are flatterers.
elegant: The magnanimous person faces great dangers willingly -- knowing that some conditions make life not worth living -- but ignores trivial ones. They confer benefits readily but are ashamed to receive them, always repaying more than they received so as to remain the superior party. They remember services given, not received -- which is why Thetis did not remind Zeus of her aid, and the Spartans cited favours received, not given. They ask for nothing, help readily, and are dignified toward the great but relaxed with ordinary people. They pursue only great deeds, are open in love and hatred, speak freely and truthfully (using irony only with the common crowd), and refuse to organize their life around anyone but a friend -- since dependence is slavish.
summary: The magnanimous person faces great dangers freely, confers benefits generously while avoiding receiving them, pursues only significant achievements, and lives with radical openness and independence. They are dignified toward equals but gentle with inferiors, and they refuse all flattery and dependence.
distilled: The magnanimous person faces great dangers willingly, gives more than they receive, speaks with radical honesty, and refuses to subordinate their life to anyone.
notes: Thetis, the sea-goddess and mother of Achilles, helped Zeus in the Iliad (I.396-406) by summoning the hundred-handed giant Briareus to protect him, yet at Iliad I.503ff she asks for a favour without mentioning past services. -- The Spartan reference is to diplomatic exchanges between Sparta and Athens; Spartan rhetorical modesty was proverbial. -- Aristotle's endorsement of irony (eirōneia) toward the vulgar connects to his discussion of truthfulness in NE IV.7 and to the Socratic tradition: Socrates was famous for irony. -- The claim that life is not worth living under certain conditions connects to NE III.1's discussion of what is voluntary under duress. -- The condemnation of flattery anticipates the fuller treatment in NE IV.6. -- This is the longest and most detailed portrait of the magnanimous person, amounting to an ideal of aristocratic virtue that profoundly influenced Western ethical thought.

refashioned:
The magnanimous person is not easily impressed, for nothing seems great to them. They do not dwell on past wrongs, for it is beneath a magnanimous person to carry grudges -- they would rather overlook them. They are not a gossip, for they will speak neither about themselves nor about others, caring neither for their own praise nor for the blame of others. For the same reason, they are not given to praising others. And they do not speak ill of people, not even their enemies -- unless from a position of deliberate superiority.

Regarding matters of necessity or triviality, they are the last person to complain or to ask for favours, for it is the mark of someone who takes such things seriously to behave that way about them.

They are the type of person who will possess beautiful and unprofitable things rather than profitable and useful ones, for this is more fitting to a character that is self-sufficient.
elegant: Nothing seems great to the magnanimous person, so they are not easily impressed. They do not hold grudges but overlook wrongs; they neither gossip, nor praise, nor blame -- not even their enemies, except from deliberate superiority. They never complain about necessities or ask favours over trifles. They prefer beautiful but unprofitable possessions over useful ones, befitting a self-sufficient character.
summary: The magnanimous person is difficult to impress, quick to forgive, and indifferent to gossip and petty complaints. They prefer what is beautiful to what is merely useful, reflecting their fundamental self-sufficiency.
distilled: The magnanimous person overlooks wrongs, avoids gossip and complaint, and values beauty over utility, reflecting a character that is fundamentally self-sufficient.
notes: The preference for 'beautiful and unprofitable things' over 'profitable and useful ones' encapsulates a core aristocratic Greek value: the truly free person transcends mere utility. -- The magnanimous person's reluctance to hold grudges contrasts with the common Greek endorsement of revenge and with the 'help friends, harm enemies' ethic visible in Homer and the tragedians. -- The self-sufficiency (autarkeia) mentioned here connects to Aristotle's account of happiness as self-sufficient in NE I.7, and to his claim that the contemplative life is the most self-sufficient in NE X.7. -- The portrait of someone who does not gossip, praise, or blame reflects an ideal of emotional and social independence rarely achieved in practice.

refashioned:
Further, a slow step is thought fitting for the magnanimous person, along with a deep voice and a steady manner of speaking. For someone who takes few things seriously is not likely to be hurried, and someone who considers nothing truly great is not likely to become agitated. A shrill voice and a hurried walk are products of urgency and excitement.

Such, then, is the magnanimous person. The one who falls short is unduly humble, and the one who goes beyond is vain. Now even these are not considered bad people -- for they are not malicious -- but merely mistaken.

The unduly humble person, being worthy of good things, deprives themselves of what they deserve. They seem to have something defective about them, stemming from their failure to think themselves worthy of what is good. They appear not to know themselves, for otherwise they would have desired the things they deserved, since these were genuine goods. Yet such people are not considered foolish, but rather excessively retiring. However, this very reputation tends to make them worse, since each kind of person aims at what corresponds to their sense of their own worth, and these people hold back even from noble actions and undertakings, considering themselves unworthy, and from external goods no less.

Vain people, on the other hand, are foolish and ignorant of themselves -- and obviously so. Not being worthy of them, they attempt honourable undertakings and are then exposed. They adorn themselves with fine clothing and outward display, and wish their good fortune to be publicly known, boasting about it as though it would win them honour.

But undue humility is more opposed to magnanimity than vanity is, for it is both more common and worse.

Magnanimity, then, is concerned with honour on the grand scale, as we have said.
elegant: A slow step, deep voice, and steady speech befit the magnanimous person -- one who is neither hurried nor agitated because few things seem important. The unduly humble fall short: being worthy of good things, they deprive themselves through self-underestimation and hold back from noble actions, growing worse as their retiring nature becomes habitual. The vain go to excess: foolish and self-ignorant, they attempt honours beyond them and are exposed, adorning themselves with outward show. Undue humility is more opposed to magnanimity than vanity, being both commoner and worse.
summary: The magnanimous person's composure -- slow step, deep voice, steady speech -- reflects their inner disposition. Undue humility is worse than vanity because it causes worthy people to deprive themselves of deserved goods and withdraw from noble action, creating a self-reinforcing cycle of diminishment.
distilled: Undue humility is worse than vanity because it causes the worthy to deprive themselves of what they deserve, creating a self-reinforcing withdrawal from noble action.
notes: The physical description of the magnanimous person (slow gait, deep voice, level speech) shows Aristotle's characteristic attention to the bodily expression of character states; compare his Physiognomics and his remarks on emotional expression in the Rhetoric. -- The claim that undue humility is self-reinforcing anticipates modern psychological concepts of self-fulfilling prophecy and imposter syndrome. -- The judgment that undue humility is 'more opposed' to magnanimity than vanity follows the pattern identified in NE II.8: the deficiency is often more contrary to the virtue than the excess, though here (unusually) the deficiency of self-assessment is considered worse. -- The vain person's love of outward show connects to broader Greek critiques of appearance versus reality, a theme central to Plato's Republic.

refashioned:
There seems to be a virtue in the sphere of honour that relates to magnanimity as generosity relates to magnificence. As we noted in our earlier remarks on the subject, neither this virtue nor generosity has anything to do with the grand scale, but both dispose us rightly with regard to moderate and everyday matters. Just as in the getting and giving of wealth there is a mean, an excess, and a deficiency, so too honour may be desired more than is right, or less, or from the right sources and in the right way.

We blame both the ambitious person -- as someone who seeks honour more than is right and from wrong sources -- and the unambitious person, who is unwilling to be honoured even for noble reasons. But sometimes we praise the ambitious person as spirited and a lover of what is noble, and the unambitious person as moderate and self-controlled, as we said in our earlier treatment.

Clearly, since 'fond of honour' has more than one meaning, we do not always apply the term 'ambitious' to the same thing. When we praise it, we are thinking of someone who loves honour more than most people do. When we blame it, we are thinking of someone who loves it more than is right.

Since this mean has no name of its own, the extremes seem to compete for its place, as if the territory were vacant. But where there is excess and deficiency, there is also an intermediate. People desire honour both more and less than they should, so it must be possible to desire it as one should. This nameless mean state is what is praised -- appearing as unambitiousness relative to ambition, and as ambition relative to unambitiousness, and in a sense as both together relative to each extreme separately.

This appears to be true of the other virtues as well, but in this case the extremes seem like direct contradictories only because the mean has not received a name.
elegant: As generosity relates to magnificence, so this unnamed virtue relates to magnanimity: both govern moderate matters rather than grand ones. In seeking honour, there is a mean between excessive ambition and insufficient ambition. We praise ambition when it means spirited pursuit of noble recognition, and blame it when honour is sought excessively or from wrong sources. Because the mean has no name, the extremes appear to be direct opposites competing for the vacant middle ground. But wherever excess and deficiency exist, an intermediate must exist too.
summary: Just as generosity is the everyday counterpart of magnificence, there is an unnamed virtue that is the everyday counterpart of magnanimity. It governs the moderate pursuit of honour. Because this mean has no name, people oscillate between praising and blaming both ambition and unambitiousness depending on context.
distilled: The unnamed virtue of properly measured ambition governs the everyday pursuit of honour, just as generosity governs everyday matters of wealth.
notes: The parallel between unnamed honour-virtue/magnanimity and generosity/magnificence creates a systematic structure: each domain (wealth, honour) has both an everyday and a grand-scale virtue. -- The problem of the unnamed mean illustrates a genuine difficulty in Aristotle's ethical theory: not every virtue corresponds to a recognized term in ordinary Greek. He notes this problem at NE II.7 as well. -- The observation that the same quality can be praised or blamed depending on context prefigures later discussions of the context-dependence of moral evaluation. -- 'Our first remarks' refers back to NE II.7 (1107b30-1108a1), where Aristotle briefly catalogues virtues including this unnamed one.

refashioned: Patience is a mean with respect to anger. The middle state has no established name, and the extremes are almost nameless as well. We place patience in the middle position, though it inclines toward the deficiency, which also lacks a name. The excess might be called a kind of 'irascibility.' For the underlying feeling is anger, while the things that provoke it are many and varied.
elegant: Patience is the mean regarding anger, though neither the mean nor the extremes have established names. The mean inclines toward deficiency. The excess could be called irascibility. The feeling involved is anger, and its provocations are many.
summary: Patience is the virtue governing anger, positioned as a mean that leans toward under-reaction rather than over-reaction. The excess is irascibility, and anger has many diverse causes.
distilled: Patience is the mean regarding anger, leaning toward restraint rather than excess, in a domain where provocations are many and varied.
notes: The Greek term praotēs is variously translated as 'patience,' 'gentleness,' 'mildness,' or 'good temper.' It connotes a composed disposition rather than mere passivity. -- Aristotle's acknowledgment that the terminology is inadequate (the mean and both extremes nearly lack names) is methodologically important: it shows that his ethical framework sometimes outstrips ordinary language. -- The observation that the mean 'inclines towards the deficiency' illustrates the principle from NE II.8 that the mean is not always equidistant from the extremes. -- The claim that anger has 'many and diverse' causes sets up the detailed analysis of varieties of anger that follows.

refashioned:
The person who is angry at the right things and with the right people, and further in the right way, at the right time, and for the right duration, is praised. This, then, will be the patient person, since patience is what we praise.

The patient person tends to be unperturbed, not led by passion, but angry in the manner, at the things, and for the length of time that right reason prescribes. However, they are thought to err rather on the side of deficiency, for the patient person is not vengeful but tends instead toward making allowances.
elegant: The patient person is angry at the right things, with the right people, in the right way, at the right time, and for the right duration. They are unperturbed and governed by reason rather than passion, though they err on the side of deficiency -- being inclined to make allowances rather than seek revenge.
summary: Patience means being angry only when appropriate -- at the right things, people, time, manner, and duration. The patient person is guided by reason rather than passion and tends to be forgiving rather than vengeful.
distilled: The patient person gets angry only when, at whom, and for as long as right reason demands, erring if anything on the side of forgiveness.
