notes: The specification of anger as 'right things, right people, right way, right time, right duration' is the most detailed application of the mean formula in the Ethics, showing the multiple dimensions along which anger can go wrong. -- 'Right reason' (orthos logos) is the standard of practical wisdom (phronesis) discussed in NE VI; the practically wise person determines the mean in each case. -- The tendency to err toward deficiency parallels the claim in NE II.9 that we should aim toward the extreme that is less opposed to the mean. -- The opposition between vengefulness and making allowances connects to broader Greek debates about justice: the tragic tradition often explores the destructive consequences of vengeful anger.

refashioned:
The deficiency -- whether we call it a kind of 'unirascibility' or something else -- is blamed. For people who fail to get angry at the things they should be angry at are considered foolish. So are those who fail to get angry in the right way, at the right time, or with the right people. Such a person is thought not to feel things or to be pained by them. Since they never get angry, they seem unlikely to defend themselves.

To endure being insulted, and to tolerate insults directed at one's friends, is slavish.
elegant: The deficiency -- a failure to feel anger when appropriate -- is blamed. Those who are not angry at what they should be, or not in the right way or at the right time, are considered foolish and insensible. They seem unlikely to defend themselves. Enduring insults to oneself or one's friends without anger is slavish.
summary: Failing to get angry when anger is warranted is a vice, not a virtue. People who never get angry appear insensible and incapable of self-defense. Tolerating insults to oneself or one's friends is a form of servility.
distilled: The inability to feel anger when it is warranted is not virtue but a slavish insensibility that leaves oneself and one's friends undefended.
notes: The condemnation of excessive passivity is notable in a chapter often read as endorsing patience: Aristotle insists that the capacity for anger is morally necessary. -- The characterization of excessive tolerance as 'slavish' (andrapodōdes) reflects the Greek association of servility with the condition of slavery, a deeply stigmatized state. -- This passage is often cited in discussions of whether pacifism is morally admirable or morally deficient by Aristotelian standards. -- The duty to defend one's friends anticipates the treatment of friendship obligations in NE VIII-IX.

refashioned:
The excess can manifest in every dimension mentioned: one can be angry with the wrong people, at the wrong things, more than is appropriate, too quickly, or for too long. Yet all these errors are not found in the same person -- indeed they could not be, for complete evil destroys even itself and becomes unbearable.

Hot-tempered people get angry quickly and with the wrong people and at the wrong things and more than is appropriate, but their anger subsides quickly -- which is the best thing about them. This happens because they do not restrain their anger but retaliate openly, thanks to their quickness of temper, and then the anger passes.

Choleric people, by contrast, are excessively quick-tempered and fly into a rage at everything and on every occasion -- hence their name.

Sulky people are hard to appease and hold onto their anger for a long time, because they repress it. The anger subsides only when they retaliate, for revenge relieves them by producing pleasure in place of pain. Without retaliation, they carry the burden indefinitely. Because their anger is not visible, no one thinks to reason with them, and working through anger internally takes a long time. Such people are the most troublesome -- to themselves and to those closest to them.

We call 'bad-tempered' those who are angry at the wrong things, more than is right, for longer than is right, and who cannot be appeased until they have inflicted retribution or punishment.
elegant: Excessive anger manifests in multiple ways: wrong targets, wrong causes, disproportionate intensity, excessive speed, and excessive duration -- though all cannot coexist, since complete vice destroys itself. Hot-tempered people anger quickly but recover fast, since they retaliate openly and the anger passes. Choleric people rage at everything on every occasion. Sulky people suppress anger, holding it until revenge produces pleasure in place of pain; without retaliation, they carry the burden silently and endlessly, tormenting themselves and those closest to them. Bad-tempered people are angry at wrong things, too intensely, too long, and cannot rest until they have punished.
summary: Excessive anger takes many forms: the hot-tempered flare up quickly but recover; the choleric rage indiscriminately; the sulky suppress their anger until revenge, tormenting themselves and others in the interim; the bad-tempered cannot be appeased without retribution.
distilled: Excessive anger takes multiple forms -- quick but passing, indiscriminate, sulkily repressed, or vindictively sustained -- each destructive in its own way.
notes: The taxonomy of anger types shows Aristotle's empirical, observational approach to ethics, resembling psychological classification. -- The claim that 'complete evil destroys itself' is a significant metaphysical principle connecting to Aristotle's view that evil is parasitic on good (compare Metaphysics IX.9). -- The distinction between those who express anger openly and those who repress it anticipates modern psychological distinctions between externalized and internalized anger. -- 'Choleric' (akrocholoi) literally means 'sharp-biled,' reflecting the Greek humoral theory that connected bile (cholē) to anger -- a connection developed further in Aristotle's biological works and later by Galen. -- The analysis of sulky people's self-torment through repressed anger has been noted as a precursor to psychoanalytic concepts.

refashioned:
We treat the excess as more opposed to patience than the deficiency, for several reasons: not only is excessive anger more common -- since the desire for revenge is more natural to humans -- but bad-tempered people are also harder to live with.

What we said in our earlier treatment of the subject is confirmed by our present analysis: it is not easy to define precisely how, with whom, at what, and for how long one should be angry, nor to determine the exact point where right action ends and wrong begins. Someone who strays a little from the ideal -- whether toward excess or deficiency -- is not blamed. Sometimes we even praise those who show the deficiency, calling them patient, and sometimes we call angry people spirited, as being capable of leadership.

How far and in what way a person must deviate before becoming blameworthy is not easy to state in a general rule, for the judgment depends on particular circumstances and on perception. But this much at least is clear: the middle state is praiseworthy -- the state in which we are angry with the right people, at the right things, in the right way, and so on -- while excesses and deficiencies are blameworthy. They are slightly blameworthy when small, more so when greater, and very much when extreme.

Evidently, then, we must hold to the middle state. Enough of the dispositions relating to anger.
elegant: Excessive anger is more opposed to patience than deficiency, being both more common (revenge is more human) and harder to live with. Precisely defining the right measure of anger -- how, with whom, at what, for how long -- is impossible in general terms; the judgment depends on particular facts and perception. Sometimes we praise those who show deficiency as patient, and sometimes those who show excess as spirited. But the middle state is clearly praiseworthy: being angry at the right things, with the right people, in the right way. Deviations are blameworthy in proportion to their degree.
summary: Excessive anger is more opposed to patience than insufficient anger, being both more natural and more socially destructive. The exact boundaries of appropriate anger cannot be stated as a universal rule but depend on perception of particular circumstances. The mean remains clearly praiseworthy.
distilled: The right measure of anger cannot be captured in a general rule but depends on the perception of particular circumstances; the mean is always praiseworthy.
notes: The claim that the decision 'depends on the particular facts and on perception' is one of the most important methodological statements in the Ethics, affirming the role of practical wisdom (phronesis) and moral perception (aisthēsis) over universal rules -- see NE II.2, II.9, and VI.8. -- 'Our earlier treatment' refers to NE II.7 (1108a4-9). -- The claim that revenge is 'more human' (anthrōpikōteron) acknowledges a natural tendency without endorsing it. -- The recognition that we sometimes praise both extremes depending on context shows the flexibility and context-sensitivity of Aristotle's ethical framework. -- This concluding section mirrors the structure of NE II.9, where Aristotle offers practical advice about aiming toward the less opposed extreme.

refashioned:
In social gatherings, in daily life, and in the exchange of words and actions, some people are thought to be obsequious. These are the ones who praise everything to give pleasure and never raise objections, thinking it their duty to cause no pain to anyone they meet. Others, by contrast, oppose everything and care nothing about causing pain -- these are called churlish and contentious.

It is plain enough that both these dispositions are blameworthy, and that the middle state is praiseworthy -- the one in which a person accepts and objects to the right things in the right way. But this middle state has no name of its own, though it most closely resembles friendliness.
elegant: In social life, the obsequious agree with everything to avoid giving pain, while the churlish oppose everything with no concern for others' feelings. Both are blameworthy. The praiseworthy mean -- accepting and objecting to the right things in the right way -- has no name, though it most resembles friendliness.
summary: Social virtue lies between obsequiousness (agreeing with everything to please) and churlishness (opposing everything without concern for others). This unnamed mean most resembles friendliness.
distilled: The unnamed social virtue between obsequious agreement and churlish contentiousness most closely resembles friendliness.
notes: This unnamed virtue is sometimes called 'friendliness' by translators, though Aristotle explicitly distinguishes it from actual friendship (philia), which he treats extensively in NE VIII-IX. -- The Greek term for 'obsequious' (areskos) connotes someone who tries to be pleasing in a morally servile way. -- 'Churlish' (dyskolos/dyseris) suggests someone who is quarrelsome and difficult to be around. -- The social virtues treated in NE IV.6-8 (friendliness, truthfulness, wit) form a group dealing with interpersonal pleasantness, distinct from the virtues concerning wealth and honour treated earlier.

refashioned:
For the person who embodies this middle state is very much like what we would call a good friend, if we added affection to the equation. But this disposition differs from friendship in that it involves no emotional attachment to one's associates. Such a person responds rightly to situations not because of love or dislike, but because of the kind of person they are.

They will behave the same way toward those they know and those they do not, toward intimates and strangers alike -- except that in each case they adjust their behavior as befits the relationship. For it is not appropriate to show the same degree of care for intimates and for strangers, nor are the conditions that make it right to cause pain the same in each case.
elegant: This middle state resembles good friendship minus the affection. The person who has it responds rightly not from love or dislike but from their own character. They treat acquaintances and strangers similarly, but adjust to each relationship, since the same degree of care and the same threshold for causing pain are not appropriate for all.
summary: This unnamed social virtue resembles friendship without the emotional bond. The person who possesses it treats people appropriately based on their relationship, responding rightly from character rather than personal attachment.
distilled: Social virtue resembles friendship without affection: it is right conduct toward others based on character, adjusted to the nature of each relationship.
notes: The distinction between this virtue and friendship proper is important: friendship (philia) requires mutual affection and goodwill (NE VIII.2), while this social virtue operates from character alone. -- The principle of adjusting behavior to the relationship anticipates the detailed discussion of different types of friendship in NE VIII.3-4. -- This passage illustrates Aristotle's view that virtue is a stable character disposition (hexis) that produces appropriate responses across varying circumstances, rather than a rule applied mechanically.

refashioned:
We have said in general terms that this person will associate with others in the right way. But it is by reference to what is honourable and expedient that they will aim at either not causing pain or contributing pleasure.

For this virtue seems to be concerned with the pleasures and pains of social life. Wherever contributing pleasure would be dishonourable or harmful, they will refuse and will choose instead to cause pain. If acquiescing in another's action would bring that person significant disgrace or injury, while opposing it would cause only a small amount of pain, they will not acquiesce but will decline.

They will associate differently with people of high standing and with ordinary people, with closer and more distant acquaintances, and similarly with respect to every other kind of social difference, giving to each group what is appropriate. While they naturally prefer to contribute pleasure and avoid causing pain, they will be guided by the consequences when these are significant -- that is, by what honour and expediency require.
elegant: This person aims at contributing pleasure or avoiding pain in accordance with what is honourable and expedient. Where contributing pleasure would be dishonourable or harmful, they refuse. Where another's action would bring significant disgrace but opposing it causes only minor pain, they oppose. They adjust their treatment to each person's standing and relationship, preferring to give pleasure but guided by the consequences when honour and expediency demand it.
summary: The socially virtuous person aims to give pleasure and avoid pain in social interactions, but they are guided by honour and expediency. They will cause minor pain to prevent serious harm, and they adjust their conduct to suit different relationships and social standings.
distilled: Social virtue means giving pleasure when possible but choosing to cause minor pain when honour or the other person's genuine good requires it.
notes: The balance between pleasantness and honesty in this account anticipates the discussion of truthfulness in NE IV.7. -- The principle that small present pains can be justified by preventing greater future harms reflects Aristotle's teleological thinking about action. -- The sensitivity to social standing and relational distance shows Aristotle's awareness of the complexity of social life -- a feature sometimes criticized as elitist but which reflects real social dynamics. -- The reference to 'honour and expediency' (to kalon kai to sympheron) pairs two of Aristotle's three fundamental categories of practical motivation (the third being pleasure).

refashioned:
For the sake of a great future pleasure, too, they will inflict small present pains.

The person who attains the mean, then, is as we have described, though they have not received a name. Among those who contribute pleasure, the person who aims at being pleasant with no ulterior motive is obsequious, while the person who does so to gain some advantage -- whether money or what money can buy -- is a flatterer. The person who objects to everything is, as we said, churlish and contentious.

The extremes seem to be contradictories of each other because the mean has received no name.
elegant: The person who attains the mean will even inflict small present pains for the sake of great future pleasures. Though unnamed, this mean is distinct from both the obsequious (pleasant without ulterior motive) and the flatterer (pleasant for profit). The extremes appear contradictory only because the mean lacks a name.
summary: The socially virtuous person will accept small present costs for greater future benefits. The obsequious person is pleasant without ulterior motive, the flatterer is pleasant for gain, and the churlish person objects to everything. The extremes appear contradictory only because the mean is unnamed.
distilled: The socially virtuous person navigates between obsequious pleasantness and churlish contentiousness, unnamed but distinct from both flattery and quarrelsomeness.
notes: The distinction between the obsequious person (pleasant without motive) and the flatterer (pleasant for profit) adds a motivational dimension to the analysis. -- The flatterer (kolax) is treated more extensively in NE IV.6 and is one of Aristotle's paradigm cases of a vice that corrupts social relationships. -- The repeated observation that unnamed means cause their extremes to appear as direct contradictories is a methodological insight: linguistic gaps can create the illusion that only two options exist where three are present. -- This pattern recurs in the virtues of ambition (4.30) and patience (4.31).

refashioned:
The mean opposed to boastfulness occupies nearly the same sphere, and it too is unnamed. It will be worthwhile to describe these states as well, for we will better understand character by examining them in detail, and we will be more convinced that the virtues are means if we see this confirmed in every case.

We have described those in social life who make the giving of pleasure or pain their object. Let us now describe those who pursue truth or falsehood -- in their words, their deeds, and the claims they make about themselves.

The boastful person is inclined to claim qualities that bring them glory, either when they do not possess them at all or when they possess them to a lesser degree than claimed. The self-deprecating person, by contrast, disclaims or belittles what they actually have. The person who observes the mean calls things by their true names, being truthful in both life and word, owning to what they have -- neither more nor less.

Each of these approaches may be adopted with or without a further purpose. But as a matter of character, each person speaks, acts, and lives in accordance with their disposition -- unless they are acting with some ulterior motive.

Falsehood is in itself base and blameworthy, while truth is noble and praiseworthy. Thus the truthful person, being in the mean, is worthy of praise, while both kinds of untruthful person are blameworthy -- the boastful person especially so.
elegant: Truthfulness, though unnamed as a formal virtue, is the mean between boastfulness and self-deprecation. The boastful claim qualities they lack or exaggerate those they have; the self-deprecating disclaim or belittle what they possess; the truthful person owns to what they are -- neither more nor less. Each person's speech and conduct reflect their character unless driven by ulterior motives. Falsehood is inherently base; truth is inherently noble. The boastful person is the more blameworthy of the two extremes.
summary: Truthfulness about oneself is a virtue -- the mean between boasting and self-deprecation. The truthful person accurately represents their qualities, while the boaster overclaims and the self-deprecator underclaims. Falsehood is inherently base, making boasting the worse vice.
distilled: Truthfulness is the virtue of representing oneself accurately -- neither overclaiming like the boaster nor underclaiming like the self-deprecator.
notes: The virtue of truthfulness (alētheia) here concerns self-presentation in everyday life, not truth in a philosophical or scientific sense. -- The Greek term for self-deprecation is eirōneia (irony), from which our word 'irony' derives. Aristotle treats it more favorably than boasting, and its association with Socrates gives it philosophical prestige. -- The claim that 'falsehood is in itself base' states a strong ethical position on lying, though Aristotle's view is more nuanced than Kant's absolute prohibition. -- This section covers the second of three social virtues (the others being friendliness and wit), all concerned with how we interact pleasantly and honestly with others.

refashioned:
Let us discuss both types, beginning with the truthful person. We are not speaking of the person who keeps their agreements -- that concerns justice and would belong to a different virtue -- but of the person who is truthful in word and life in matters where nothing of that sort is at stake, simply because such is their character.

Such a person would seem to be equitable as a matter of fact. For someone who loves truth and is honest when nothing is at stake will be even more honest when something is. They will avoid falsehood as something base, having trained themselves to avoid it even when it costs them nothing. Such a person is worthy of praise.

The truthful person inclines rather toward understatement, for this seems to be in better taste, since exaggerations are tiresome.
elegant: The truthfulness in question concerns self-presentation, not contractual fidelity (which belongs to justice). The person who is honest when nothing is at stake will be even more so when something is, avoiding falsehood as inherently base. Such a person tends toward understatement, which is in better taste than exaggeration.
summary: The truthful person is honest about themselves as a matter of character, not obligation. Since they avoid falsehood even when nothing is at stake, they are even more reliable when something is. They prefer understatement to exaggeration.
distilled: The person who is honest when nothing is at stake will be more honest when something is, and will prefer understatement to exaggeration.
notes: The distinction between truthfulness as a character trait and truth-keeping as a matter of justice is important: the former concerns self-presentation, the latter contractual fidelity (treated in Book V). -- The preference for understatement connects to Aristotle's favorable treatment of eirōneia (irony/understatement) in the magnanimity chapter (4.27) and to the Greek ideal of sophrosyne (moderation/self-restraint). -- The claim that exaggerations are 'tiresome' reflects an aesthetic as well as moral judgment -- a recurring feature of Aristotle's ethical evaluations. -- 'Equitable' (epieikēs) carries a specific technical meaning in NE V.10, where it refers to a correction of strict legal justice; here it is used more loosely to mean 'fair-minded.'

refashioned:
The person who overclaims without any ulterior motive is a contemptible sort -- for otherwise they would not have taken delight in falsehood itself -- but they seem futile rather than truly bad. When someone overclaims for a specific purpose, the one who does so for reputation or honour is not greatly blameworthy as boasters go. But the one who does it for money or material gain is a more objectionable character.

What makes someone a boaster is not their ability to deceive but their deliberate purpose. It is their settled character and their choice to be that kind of person that makes them a boaster -- just as one person is a liar because they enjoy lying itself, while another lies because they want reputation or profit.

Those who boast for the sake of reputation claim qualities that attract praise or admiration. But those whose aim is gain claim qualities that are valuable to others and whose absence is hard to detect -- for instance, the powers of a prophet, a sage, or a physician. This is why most people who boast claim expertise in such areas, for these are the domains where the qualities just mentioned are found.
elegant: The purposeless boaster is contemptible but merely futile. Among purposeful boasters, those seeking honour are less blameworthy than those seeking profit. What makes someone a boaster is not their capacity for deceit but their deliberate choice to live that way -- as some lie from enjoyment, others from desire for reputation or gain. Those who boast for reputation claim admirable qualities; those who boast for gain claim expertise that is hard to verify -- prophecy, wisdom, medicine -- which is why most boasting clusters in such domains.
summary: Boasting varies in seriousness depending on its motive. Purposeless boasting is contemptible but trivial. Boasting for honour is relatively mild, but boasting for material gain is the ugliest form. Boasters for profit tend to claim hard-to-verify expertise like prophecy or medicine.
distilled: What makes someone a boaster is not the capacity to deceive but the choice to live by exaggeration, with boasting for profit being worse than boasting for reputation.
notes: The distinction between lying for its own sake and lying for an end parallels the distinction in NE VII between incontinence of desire and incontinence of spirited emotion. -- The list of hard-to-verify expertise (seer, sage, physician) reflects real social concerns in ancient Greece, where itinerant practitioners of these arts were common and their competence difficult to assess. -- Plato's dialogues (especially the Sophist and Hippias Minor) extensively explore the relationship between expertise, pretension, and deception. -- The emphasis on purpose (prohairesis) as the defining element of character echoes NE III.2 and VI.2, where deliberate choice is identified as the most revealing indicator of moral character.

refashioned:
Self-deprecating people, who understate their qualities, seem more attractive in character, for they appear to speak not for gain but to avoid ostentation. Here too, it is especially qualities that bring reputation that they disclaim -- as Socrates used to do.

Those who disclaim trivial and obvious qualities are called humbugs and are more contemptible. Sometimes this kind of false modesty actually looks like boasting -- like the Spartan style of dress; for both extreme excess and conspicuous deficiency can serve as forms of display.

But those who use understatement with moderation, understating about matters that are not too glaringly obvious, seem attractive.

It is the boaster who seems to be the true opposite of the truthful person, for they are the worse character.
elegant: Self-deprecating people seem more attractive, since they appear to speak from modesty rather than for gain -- especially when they disclaim qualities that bring prestige, as Socrates did. But disclaiming trivial or obvious qualities is contemptible humbug, sometimes amounting to a form of boasting -- like the ostentatiously austere Spartan dress, where conspicuous deficiency becomes its own display. Moderate understatement about non-obvious matters is genuinely attractive. The boaster is the true opposite of the truthful person, being the worse character.
summary: Self-deprecation is generally more attractive than boasting, especially when it involves genuine modesty about significant qualities. But disclaiming trivial things is a form of humbug, and conspicuous self-denial can become its own kind of display. The boaster is the true opposite of truthfulness.
distilled: Moderate self-deprecation about significant qualities is attractive, but conspicuous self-denial about trivial things is itself a form of boasting.
notes: The reference to Socrates' habitual self-deprecation connects to the tradition of 'Socratic irony' (eirōneia), where Socrates would profess ignorance while exposing others' pretensions. Aristotle discusses Socrates' irony more critically in the Rhetoric and elsewhere. -- The Spartan reference concerns the famous Spartan simplicity of dress (the rough tribōn or cloak), which other Greeks sometimes viewed as an inverted form of vanity -- ostentatious in its very plainness. Plutarch and Thucydides both note this phenomenon. -- The insight that conspicuous deficiency can be a form of display anticipates modern analyses of 'competitive asceticism' and the paradoxes of anti-consumerism. -- The judgment that the boaster is 'the worse character' and thus the true opposite follows the principle from NE II.8 that the more opposed vice is typically the more harmful one.

refashioned:
Since life includes rest as well as activity, and rest includes leisure and amusement, there seems to be a kind of social exchange here too that has its own standard of taste. There is such a thing as saying -- and listening to -- the right things in the right way. The kind of people one is speaking or listening to also makes a difference.

Here too there is clearly an excess and a deficiency compared with the mean. Those who carry humour to excess are considered vulgar buffoons. They strain after laughs at all costs, aiming to get a reaction rather than to say what is becoming or to avoid hurting the target of their humour. Those who can neither make a joke themselves nor tolerate those who do are considered boorish and unpolished.

But those who joke tastefully are called witty -- a term that suggests a kind of nimbleness, a readiness to turn. For such remarks are thought to be movements of character, and just as bodies are judged by their movements, so too are characters.

However, the ridiculous is never far to seek, and most people delight in humour and jest more than they should. This is why even buffoons are sometimes called witty, since they are found entertaining. But that they differ from the truly witty person -- and substantially so -- is clear from what we have said.
elegant: Life requires rest and amusement as well as activity, and social amusement has its own standard of taste. Those who push humour to excess are vulgar buffoons, straining for laughs without regard for decorum or the feelings of their targets. Those who cannot joke or tolerate jokes are boorish. The witty person jokes tastefully, with a nimbleness that reveals character -- for just as bodies are known by their movements, characters are known by their wit. Most people enjoy humour too much, which is why buffoons are sometimes mistaken for the truly witty, but the difference is substantial.
summary: Wit is the virtue of tasteful humour in social life, positioned between buffoonery (excessive, indiscriminate humour) and boorishness (inability to joke or enjoy jokes). Most people overindulge in humour, which blurs the line between buffoons and the genuinely witty.
distilled: Wit is the virtue of tasteful humour -- a nimbleness of character distinct from the buffoon's indiscriminate jokes and the boor's inability to enjoy them.
notes: The Greek term eutrapelos ('well-turning,' translated as 'witty' or 'ready-witted') literally connotes flexibility and agility of mind. -- The analogy between bodily movement and character is characteristically Aristotelian; he develops similar connections in the Physiognomics and the Rhetoric. -- The distinction between buffoonery and genuine wit is not merely aesthetic but moral: the buffoon causes pain and violates decorum, while the witty person respects social boundaries. -- The observation that most people enjoy humour too much shows Aristotle's awareness that the mean is not where most people naturally fall. -- This is the third of the social virtues (after friendliness and truthfulness), completing the treatment of how we interact pleasantly with others.

refashioned:
Tact also belongs to the middle state. It is the mark of a tactful person to say and listen to such things as befit someone of good character and breeding. For there are some things that are fitting for such a person to say and hear in jest, and the jesting of a well-bred person differs from that of a vulgar one, just as the humour of an educated person differs from that of an uneducated one. One can see this even from the difference between the old and the new comedies: for the authors of the former found indecent language amusing, while those of the latter prefer innuendo -- and these differ considerably in terms of propriety.

Should we define the person who jokes well as one who says nothing unbecoming to a person of good character? Or as one who causes no pain, or even gives delight, to the listener? But surely this latter definition is too vague, since different things are hateful or pleasant to different people.

The kind of jokes such a person will listen to will match the kind they make, for the jokes one can put up with are also the kind one seems to endorse. There are, then, jokes they will not make, for a jest is a kind of mockery, and there are things that lawmakers forbid us to mock. Perhaps they should have forbidden jesting about such things as well.

The refined and well-bred person, therefore, will be as we have described -- being, as it were, a law to themselves.
elegant: Tact means saying and hearing in jest what befits a person of good character. The well-bred person's humour differs from the vulgar person's, as the old comedies (which found indecency amusing) differ from the new (which prefer innuendo). The well-judged joke neither disgraces the speaker nor pains the listener -- though what pleases or pains varies between individuals. Since jests are a form of mockery, and some targets are legally protected from abuse, the refined person serves as their own law regarding what may be jested about.
summary: Tact in humour means joking in a way befitting good character. The refined person's humour resembles the shift from old comedy's crude jokes to new comedy's subtle innuendo. Since jests are a form of mockery, the person of good character is their own law about what may be mocked.
distilled: The person of refined wit serves as their own law regarding humour, jesting in ways that befit good character and cause no undeserved pain.
notes: The contrast between Old and New Comedy is historically significant. Old Comedy (exemplified by Aristophanes, c. 446-386 BCE) was characterized by obscenity, personal attacks, and political satire. New Comedy (exemplified by Menander, c. 342-290 BCE) favored character-based humour and innuendo. -- The idea that the well-bred person is 'a law to themselves' (nomos heautō) is a striking formulation suggesting that the truly virtuous person has internalized the standards of right conduct. This concept influenced later natural law theory. -- The mention of lawmakers forbidding certain kinds of mockery reflects actual Athenian legislation: laws against defamation (kakēgoria) regulated public speech. -- Aristotle discusses comedy's relationship to character more extensively in the Poetics (Ch. 5).

refashioned:
Such, then, is the person who observes the mean, whether we call them tactful or witty. The buffoon, on the other hand, is a slave to their own sense of humour. They spare neither themselves nor others if they can raise a laugh, saying things that no person of refinement would say, and some that such a person would not even listen to.

The boor, in contrast, is useless for social interaction: they contribute nothing and find fault with everything.

But relaxation and amusement are thought to be a necessary element in life.
elegant: The witty person observes the mean. The buffoon is enslaved to humour, sparing no one -- not even themselves -- and saying things the refined would neither say nor hear. The boor contributes nothing and objects to everything. Yet relaxation and amusement are necessary parts of life.
summary: The witty person holds the mean between the buffoon, who is enslaved to humour and will say anything for a laugh, and the boor, who contributes nothing to social enjoyment. Both extremes are defective, since amusement is a necessary part of life.
distilled: The buffoon is enslaved to humour and the boor is hostile to it, but amusement is a necessary part of human life that requires its own virtue.
notes: The characterization of the buffoon as a 'slave' (doulos) to humour uses the strongest possible language of unfreedom, connecting comic excess to a loss of autonomy. -- The claim that amusement is 'necessary' echoes NE X.6, where Aristotle discusses the role of relaxation and pleasure in the good life. -- The boor (agroikos, literally 'rustic') connotes someone lacking the social polish of urban life -- an important cultural distinction in ancient Athens. -- The brief treatment of the boor suggests that excessive seriousness or social withdrawal is as much a vice as excessive joking, reflecting Aristotle's balanced view of human sociality.

refashioned: The means in social life that we have described are three in number, and all concern some kind of exchange of words and actions. They differ, however, in that one is concerned with truth, while the other two are concerned with pleasure. Of those concerned with pleasure, one is displayed in jesting, the other in the general social interactions of everyday life.
elegant: Three social virtues have been described, all concerning the exchange of words and actions. One governs truth; two govern pleasure -- one in jesting, the other in everyday social interaction.
summary: The three social virtues are truthfulness (concerned with honest self-presentation), wit (concerned with pleasure in jesting), and friendliness (concerned with pleasure in everyday social interaction).
distilled: The three social virtues govern truth in self-presentation, pleasure in jest, and pleasure in everyday interaction.
notes: This summary passage organizes the three social virtues treated in NE IV.6-8 into a systematic schema: friendliness (IV.6), truthfulness (IV.7), and wit (IV.8). -- The organizing principle -- one concerned with truth, two with pleasure -- provides the rational structure behind what might otherwise appear to be an arbitrary list. -- All three virtues are unnamed (or nearly so), which Aristotle notes as a recurring difficulty. -- The treatment of social virtues as a group is distinctive to Aristotle among ancient ethicists and reflects his keen awareness that human flourishing is essentially social (the 'political animal' of Politics I.2).

refashioned: Shame should not be classified as a virtue, for it is more like a feeling than a state of character. It is defined as a kind of fear of dishonour, and it produces effects similar to those produced by the fear of danger. People who feel disgraced blush, and people who fear death turn pale. Both reactions seem to be bodily conditions, which is considered characteristic of feelings rather than of settled states of character.
elegant: Shame is not a virtue but a feeling -- a fear of dishonour. Like the fear of death, it produces bodily responses: the disgraced blush as the frightened turn pale. Such bodily reactions are characteristic of feelings, not of settled character states.
summary: Shame is a feeling rather than a virtue. It is a form of fear -- specifically fear of dishonour -- and like other fears, it manifests in bodily reactions such as blushing, which marks it as an emotion rather than a stable character trait.
distilled: Shame is not a virtue but an emotion -- a fear of dishonour that manifests in bodily reactions like blushing.
notes: The distinction between feelings (pathē) and states of character (hexeis) is foundational to Aristotle's ethics; see NE II.5, where he argues that virtues are not feelings but dispositions to feel and act in the right way. -- The observation that shame produces blushing reflects Aristotle's interest in the bodily manifestations of emotion, developed more fully in De Anima and the Rhetoric (II.6). -- The placement of shame at the end of Book IV, after the virtues proper, signals its anomalous status in Aristotle's scheme. -- Aristotle's treatment of shame influenced the medieval distinction between verecundia (healthy shame) and pudor (unhealthy shame).

refashioned:
The feeling of shame is not appropriate to every age, but only to youth. We think young people should be prone to shame because they live by feeling and therefore commit many errors, which shame helps to restrain. We praise young people who are prone to shame, but no one would praise an older person for being easily embarrassed, since we believe they should not be doing anything that warrants embarrassment.

For shame is not even characteristic of a good person, since it arises from bad actions -- and such actions should not be done in the first place. Whether the actions are genuinely disgraceful or merely considered so by common opinion makes no difference: neither kind should be done, and so no occasion for shame should arise. It is the mark of a bad person even to be the kind of person who would do anything disgraceful.

To be so constituted as to feel disgraced if one does such things, and to think oneself good on that account, is absurd. For shame is felt for voluntary actions, and the good person will never voluntarily do what is bad.

Shame might be called conditionally good: if a good person were to do something disgraceful, they would feel shame. But the virtues are not subject to such conditional qualifications. And if shamelessness -- not being ashamed of doing base things -- is bad, it does not follow that being ashamed of doing them is good.

Similarly, continence is not a virtue but a mixed sort of state. This will be discussed later.

Now, however, let us turn to justice.
elegant: Shame befits youth, not maturity. The young live by feeling, commit many errors, and are rightly restrained by shame. But an older person should not be doing things that cause shame. Shame is not characteristic of the good person, who should never do anything disgraceful -- whether truly or merely by reputation. Finding it absurd to think oneself good because one would feel shame after wrongdoing: the good person simply does not voluntarily do wrong. Shame is conditionally good -- useful if one does err -- but virtue admits no such conditions. That shamelessness is bad does not make shame good. Similarly, continence is not virtue but a mixed state, to be discussed later.
summary: Shame is appropriate for the young, who err through inexperience, but not for mature adults, who should not be doing shameful things in the first place. Being disposed to feel shame after wrongdoing does not make one good -- it merely indicates one would recognize the wrongness of actions one should not be performing at all.
distilled: Shame is fitting for the young but not the mature: the truly good person has no occasion for shame because they do not voluntarily do what is disgraceful.
notes: The claim that shame is appropriate to youth but not maturity reflects a developmental view of moral psychology: the young need external and emotional checks on behaviour that the mature should have internalized as stable virtues. -- The argument that 'if shamelessness is bad, shame is not therefore good' is a logical point: two contraries can both be bad if the virtuous state transcends the opposition entirely. -- The brief mention of continence (enkrateia) as 'not virtue but a mixed state' points forward to the extended treatment in NE VII.1-10, where Aristotle distinguishes the continent person (who has bad desires but resists them) from the truly virtuous person (whose desires are already rightly ordered). -- The closing transition to justice sets up Book V, the longest book of the Ethics. -- Aristotle's treatment of shame influenced the medieval virtue of verecundia and modern discussions of the role of moral emotions in ethical life.
